name: shps
documentation:
# shps

Shape functions, clean and simple.

frame
    mesh
    state
    shape
    bases
    gauss
    nodes

rotor

rigid

plane
    shape
solid

functions.name: shps.rotor.normalized
documentation:
Return normalized vector.

```
param a

vector

param order

normalization order

returns

normalized input
```

functions.name: shps.rotor.hat
documentation:
Get the skew symmetric matrix of a rotational vector.

Parameters
----------
vector: numpy.ndarray
    the axial vector.

Returns
-------
matrix:
    the skew symmetric matrix representing the rotational vector.

functions.name: shps.rotor.vee
documentation:
Get the rotational vector representing a skew symmetric matrix.

Parameters
----------
matrix: numpy.ndarray
    the skew symmetric matrix.

Returns
-------
vector: numpy.ndarray
    the axial vector.

functions.name: shps.rotor.exp
documentation:
Evaluate the exponential of a skew symmetric matrix given its axial vector.

Parameters
----------
axial: np.ndarray
    the rotational vector.

Returns
-------
rotation_matrix:
    the rotation matrix.

functions.name: shps.rotor.log
documentation:
Evaluate the logarithm on SO3 and return the axial vector representation.

Parameters
----------
matrix: np.ndarray
    the rotation matrix.

Returns
-------
axial:
    axial vector of the logarithm.

functions.name: shps.rotor.dlog
documentation:
Compute the differential of the logarithm.

Parameters
----------
vector: numpy.ndarray
    the rotational vector.

Returns
-------
numpy.ndarray:
    the differential of the logarithm.

functions.name: shps.rotor.dexp
documentation:
Compute the exponential differential.

Parameters
----------
rotational_vector: numpy.ndarray
    the rotation vector.

Returns
-------
T_s: numpy.ndarray
    the exponential differential.

functions.name: shps.rotor.spurrier
documentation: Form a quaternion representation of the matrix R by Spurrier's algorithm.

functions.name: shps.rotor.ber
documentation:
Compute eta and mu.

Parameters
----------
angle: float
    the angle of the rotation.

Returns
-------
The first coefficient eta: float.
The second coefficient mu: float.

functions.name: shps.rotor.conversions._get_pyramid_order
documentation:
Get order of the coordinates.

Depending on the pyramid in which the point is located, the order need to be adjusted.

Parameters
----------
xyz : numpy.ndarray
   coordinates of a point on a uniform refinable grid on a ball or
   in a uniform refinable cubical grid.

References
----------
D. Roşca et al., Modelling and Simulation in Materials Science and Engineering 22:075013, 2014
https://doi.org/10.1088/0965-0393/22/7/075013

functions.name: shps.gauss.iquad
documentation:
Parameters:
    n: number of sampling points

    deg: degree

Examples:

Midpoint rule:
    xi, wi =  quad_points(deg=1, rule='mid')

Trapezoidal rule:
    xi, wi =  quad_points(deg=1, even=True)

Composite trapezoidal rule:
    xi, wi = quad_points(n=4, deg=1, rule='cotes')

Composite Simpson's rule
    xi, wi = quad_points(n=6, deg=2, rule='cotes')

Gauss-legendre
    xi, wi = quad_points(n=4, rule='legendre')

'closed': True if endpoints are included
'deg': degree of highest order polynomial exactly integrated
'n0': minimum number of points ; For Newton-Cotes rules, this is the 
      number of points for the base (non-composite rule)

functions.name: shps.gauss.newton_cotes
documentation:
Return weights and error coefficient for Newton-Cotes integration.

# Parameters

rn

: The integer order for equally-spaced data or the relative positions of
  the samples with the first sample at 0 and the last at N, where N+1 is
  the length of `rn`. N is the order of the Newton-Cotes integration.

equal

: Set to True to enforce equally spaced data.

# Returns

an

: 1-D array of weights to apply to the function at the provided sample
  positions.

B

: Error coefficient.

# Examples

Compute the integral of sin(x) in \$[0, pi]\$:
\>>> def f(x):
... return np.sin(x)
\>>> a, b = 0, np.pi
\>>> N = 2
\>>> x = np.linspace(a, b, N + 1)
\>>> an, B = newton_cotes(N, 1)
\>>> dx = (b - a) / N
\>>> quad = dx * np.sum(an * f(x))

# Notes

An N-point Newton-Cotes formula for an integral on the
interval between \$x_0\$ and \$x_N\$ is:

\$\$
int\_\{x_0}^\{x_N} f(x)dx = Delta x sum\_{i=0}^\{N} a_i f(x_i)
\+ B_N (Delta x)^\{N+2} f^\{N+1} (xi)
\$\$

where \$xi in [x_0,x_N]\$ and \$Delta x = frac\{x_N-x_0}\{N}\$ is
the average samples spacing.
If the samples are equally-spaced and N is even, then the error
term is \$B_N (Delta x)^\{N+3} f^\{N+2}(xi)\$.

Normally, the Newton-Cotes rules are used on smaller integration
regions and a composite rule is used to return the total integral.

functions.name: shps.curve.line_mesh
documentation: Create line mesh from coordinate A to B.

functions.name: shps.curve.n_point_mesh
documentation:
Create a mesh from a list of points by connecting them in a sequence
(P1 -- P2 -- P3 -- ... -- PN).

# Parameters:
points ...................... points in 3D
n_elements .................. a list containing the number of elements for each segment
order ....................... element order (polynomial interpolation order)
material .................... dictionary with material properties
reference_vector ............ a vector to define the orientation of the cross-section
dual_basis_functions ........ a boolean saying if the Lagrange multiplier field should be interpolated with dual shape functions or with Lagrange polynomials
